# **Donation Inventory & Trafficking Survivor Resource Management System** **(AKA CareTrack)**

**CS 408 – Project Specification**  
[**http://54.245.50.134**](http://54.245.50.134)   
 **Tiernan Benner**  
 **March 1, 2026**

---

# **Overview**

This project is a full-stack web application designed to manage physical donations for a nonprofit organization which I came up with the idea by wanting to potentially use this project for my mother to use who works with the organization C.O.B.S. The system replaces spreadsheet-based tracking with a centralized, secure, role-based web platform hosted on AWS EC2.

The application will allow staff members to track donations, manage inventory, assign items to survivors, and coordinate requests from volunteers and donors. The long-term goal is to extend this application for real-world use by Community Outreach Behavioral Services (COBS), though the initial version will be built as a generalized system suitable for any nonprofit distributing physical goods.

This system will meet all minimum project requirements, including:

* Landing page with navigation

* Shared header and footer

* Database-backed form submission

* Data retrieval and display

* Delete functionality

* Conditional data retrieval (search, filter, pagination)

* At least four styled pages

* AWS EC2 deployment with setup scripts

The application demonstrates a complete full-stack implementation with authentication, database integration, image handling, and deployment configuration.

---

# **Target Audience**

This application serves multiple user groups with clearly defined roles.

## **Primary Users – Staff Members (Administrators)**

Staff are the primary users of the system. They will have full Create, Read, Update, and Delete (CRUD) capabilities. Staff members can:

* Approve donations

* Assign items to survivors

* View survivor identity information

* Manage inventory

* Approve volunteer change requests

* Manage user roles and permissions

## **Secondary Users**

### **Volunteers**

Volunteers can:

* View inventory

* Submit requests for corrections or updates

* Suggest changes that staff must approve

### **Survivors**

Survivors can:

* View available inventory

* Mark items they are interested in

* Submit item requests

Survivors will not see donor identity unless the donor chooses not to remain anonymous.

### **Donors**

Donors can:

* Submit donation forms

* Upload optional item images

* Indicate whether they prefer anonymity

### **Public Users (No Login Required)**

Public users may:

* View Available Inventory

* View Requested Items

The system fills a niche for smaller nonprofits that lack dedicated IT infrastructure and currently rely on spreadsheets or manual tracking.

---

# **Functionality**

CRUD Operations Overview:

Create

* Donors submit donation forms  
* Survivors submit item requests  
* Staff add inventory entries

Read

* Users view available inventory and requested items  
* Staff view survivor information and distribution history  
* Search, filtering, and pagination allow conditional retrieval

Update

* Staff edit inventory entries  
* Staff update request status (pending → approved/denied)  
* Staff assign items to survivors

Delete

* Staff remove inventory entries  
* Staff deny or remove requests when necessary

The application will include the following primary pages:

## **1\. Landing Page**

* Overview of the system

* Navigation to all other pages

* Login and registration options

* Shared header and footer

## **2\. Available Inventory**

* Displays items currently held

* Paginated (25 items per page)

* Search by item name

* Filter by availability

* Survivors can mark interest

* Staff can edit or delete items

## **3\. Distributed Items**

* Displays historical record of assigned items

* Searchable by date

* Survivor identity visible only to staff

## **4\. Requested Items**

* Displays items currently needed by the organization

* Donors may respond by submitting a donation form

## **5\. Staff Dashboard (Role-Protected)**

* View survivor details

* Approve users

* Approve donations

* Assign items to survivors

* Manage change requests

---

## **Core Requirement Implementation**

The application satisfies the assignment requirements by including:

* Shared header and footer across all pages

* Database-backed forms (donation submission)

* Ability to retrieve and display entries from the database

* Delete functionality (staff-only)

* Conditional retrieval using:

  * Pagination

  * Search

  * Status filtering

  * Date filtering

* Styling via Bootstrap

* AWS EC2 backend deployment

---

# **Database Schema**

The application will initially use SQLite for simplicity and ease of deployment.

## **Users Table**

* id (Primary Key)

* username

* email

* password\_hash

* role (public, donor, survivor, volunteer, staff)

* is\_approved (boolean)

## **Donors Table**

* id (Primary Key)

* name

* email

* phone

* anonymous (boolean)

## **Items Table**

* id (Primary Key)

* name

* description

* category

* storage\_location

* image\_path

* donor\_id (Foreign Key)

* assigned\_to (Foreign Key, nullable)

* status (available, distributed)

* created\_date

## **Survivors Table**

* id (Primary Key)

* name

* contact\_info (visible to staff only)

## **Requests Table**

* id (Primary Key)

* user\_id (Foreign Key)

* item\_id (Foreign Key)

* request\_date

* status (pending, approved, denied)

Each item will be stored individually. Quantity tracking will not be implemented to avoid ambiguity between similar but non-identical items.

---

# **Media**

Item images will be optionally uploaded by donors or added by staff.

* Maximum upload size: 2MB

* Images stored in an /uploads directory on EC2

* Images resized and compressed using Pillow

* Placeholder image used when no image is provided (generic N/A image)  
  * Placeholder images will be sourced from public domain or permissively licensed image repositories

Supported image formats will include JPEG and PNG. Images will be validated for file type and size before upload to prevent unsupported or potentially unsafe files from being stored on the server.

This ensures reasonable storage use while still allowing visual identification of items.

---

# **Wireframes**

Wireframes will be hand-drawn on an iPad and inserted into the Google Document.

Planned wireframes include:

* Requests Page (Staff only)

* Donation Page

* Requested Items Page

* Distributed Items Page

* Available Inventory Page

* Landing Page

* Login Page

Each wireframe will clearly show:

* Header navigation

* Main content area

* Footer

* Search bar placement

* Pagination controls

  ![][image1]![][image2]![][image3]![][image4]![][image5]![][image6]  
  ![][image7]

---

**Technology Stack**

The application will use a Python-based full-stack architecture built around the Django web framework.

**Backend**

* Backend Language: Python  
* Backend Framework: Django  
* Web Server (development): Django development server  
* Production Server: Gunicorn behind Nginx  
* Database: SQLite

**Frontend**

* Templates: Django Template Engine (server-side rendering)  
* Styling: Bootstrap 5  
* Static files: Django static file system

**Authentication and Security**

Django’s built-in authentication system will be used for:

* User registration and login  
* Role-based permissions  
* Password hashing using Django’s secure password hashing system

**Media Handling**

Item images will be processed and resized using Pillow before being stored on the server.

This stack was selected because Django provides an integrated framework including authentication, ORM database models, form handling, and security features while remaining fully compatible with the AWS EC2 deployment environment.

---

# **EC2 Setup Scripts**

## **Installation Script (install.sh)**

This script will:

* Update the system

* Install Python3 and pip

* Install Nginx

* Install virtualenv

* Install required packages from requirements.txt

* Configure firewall rules

## **Configuration Script (configure.sh)**

This script will:

* Create and activate virtual environment

* Initialize database

* Configure Nginx reverse proxy

* Start Gunicorn server

* Enable systemd service for persistence

---

# **Installation and Deployment Instructions**

1. Launch Ubuntu EC2 instance.

2. SSH into the instance.

3. Clone the project repository.

4. Run setup.sh.

5. Access application via EC2 public IP address.

---

# **Automated Testing**

Testing will be implemented using Pytest.

Test coverage will include:

* User registration and login

* Role-based access restrictions

* Form submission and database insertion

* Staff-only page protection

* Search and filter functionality

* Deletion functionality

All tests will be executed locally before deployment.

---

# **Project Schedule (7 Checkpoints)**

## **Checkpoint 1 (Week 1\)**

* Finalize requirements

* Complete database schema draft

* Complete all wireframes

## **Checkpoint 2 (Week 2\)**

* Implement Django models

* Configure Django project and app structure

* Create base template with shared header and footer

## **Checkpoint 3 (Week 3\)**

* Implement authentication

* Role-based permissions functional

## **Checkpoint 4 (Week 4\)**

* Donation form functional

* CRUD functionality operational

* Local testing complete

## **Checkpoint 5 (Week 5\)**

* Pagination implemented

* Search and filtering working

* Image upload functional

## **Checkpoint 6 (Week 6\)**

* Deploy to AWS EC2

* Installation scripts verified

* Nginx configured properly

## **Checkpoint 7 (Week 7–8)**

* Automated testing finalized

* User interface refinement

* Documentation complete

* Deployment stable and demo-ready

---

# **Conclusion**

This project fulfills all minimum full-stack requirements while also providing a meaningful real-world solution that can be extended beyond the classroom. It demonstrates:

* Full CRUD operations

* Role-based authentication

* AWS EC2 deployment

* Database integration

* Conditional retrieval

* Media handling

* Automated testing

* Structured development planning

The system is practical, scalable, and should be suitable for nonprofit operational use after further refinement.

## **Summary of Changes**

Peer review feedback was evaluated and several small improvements were made to clarify parts of the proposal while keeping the overall design unchanged. First, a short CRUD operations section was added to explicitly describe how Create, Read, Update, and Delete operations map to the system’s functionality. While the original proposal already implied these operations through the described features and pages, explicitly listing them improves clarity and directly connects the functionality to database interactions.  
Second, the media section was slightly expanded to provide additional implementation detail. The updated description now specifies supported image formats and basic validation checks that will occur before files are stored on the server. This ensures the system clearly defines how uploaded media will be handled.  
Some peer comments suggested that wireframes were missing or that deployment scripts should already exist. After reviewing the document, these elements were already addressed appropriately within the context of a project specification. The wireframe sketches are included in the document contrary to the review, they must have just missed them is all, or expected a different format than my handdrawn versions. As for the review by Zach criticizing the lack of EC2 installation scripts in my repo, this Project Specification is obviously a future plan, not a description of something that already exists yet, so they are described as planned components rather than completed artifacts, so his comments are to be ignored. Because this document represents a development plan rather than a finished implementation, no changes were necessary for those sections.  
Finally, feedback from Zach suggesting the project scope was too large was considered. However, I think that this project is doable in the time and other reviewers indicated that the scope and schedule are realistic for the eight-week timeline as well, and the structured checkpoints already provide a clear development path. As a result, just as with the EC2 installation scripts descriptions, the scope of the project remains unchanged.
